ABW: This island lies just south of the Caribbean hurricane belt, so direct hurricane hits are rare enough that locals can usually name the handful that happened from memory.
AFG: This country has changed its national flag more than 20 times since 1901, reportedly more than any other country in modern history — a reflection of just how many governments have risen and fallen there.
AGO: Despite being one of Africa's top oil producers, this country still imports most of its refined gasoline because it has only one working refinery.
AIA: This island has no rivers, lakes, or streams at all — every drop of fresh water comes from rain catchment or desalination.
ALA: This autonomous territory belongs to Finland, though nearly everyone there speaks Swedish, and it issues its own postage stamps and vehicle plates.
ALB: This country built over 170,000 concrete bunkers under a Cold War-era regime for a population of under 3 million — more per capita than any other country.
AND: This country has one of the highest life expectancies in the world, credited partly to its mountain air and highly active population.
ARE: This country's police force patrols in a fleet that includes a Bugatti Veyron and a Lamborghini Aventador, marketed as the world's fastest police cars.
ARG: This country's capital is home to 9 de Julio Avenue, often called the widest avenue on Earth — wide enough to take several traffic-light cycles to cross on foot.
ARM: This country was the first in the world to adopt Christianity as a state religion, in the year 301 AD.
ASM: Its residents are U.S. nationals but not U.S. citizens by birth — a unique legal status shared by no U.S. state.
ATA: This continent has no native population, no time zone of its own, and no country situated there — it's governed instead by an international treaty.
ATF: This territory has no permanent residents at all — only rotating scientists stationed on its remote subantarctic islands.
ATG: This country's tourism board famously claims '365 beaches, one for every day of the year' — a promotional line that's stuck for decades, even though the exact count depends on how you define a beach.
AUS: More than a third of this country's landmass lies within the tropics, yet most of its population lives along the temperate southeastern coast.
AUT: The Christmas carol 'Silent Night' was written in this country and first performed in a small village church in 1818.
AZE: This country's name is thought to mean 'land of fire,' inspired by natural gas seeps that have burned continuously from its hillsides for millennia.
BDI: This country is one of the least electrified on Earth — most of its population has never had access to grid electricity.
BEL: This country has three official languages — Dutch, French, and German — despite being smaller than the U.S. state of Maryland.
BEN: This country is considered the birthplace of Vodun, which remains an officially recognized religion practiced by a large share of the population.
BFA: This country's name means 'land of upright/honest people,' adopted after independence to replace its colonial name, Upper Volta.
BGD: This country is criss-crossed by hundreds of rivers and tributaries, giving it one of the densest river networks of any country on Earth — estimates of the total range from roughly 230 major rivers up to 700 once every tributary is counted.
BGR: People here traditionally nod their head for 'no' and shake it side to side for 'yes' — the reverse of most of the world.
BHR: This country's name means 'two seas' in Arabic, referring to natural underground layers of fresh and salt water once found around the island.
BHS: This country's Exuma islands are home to a colony of wild pigs that swim out to greet passing boats, with no confirmed explanation for how they got there.
BIH: This country's capital is nicknamed the 'Jerusalem of Europe' for its cluster of mosques, churches, and a synagogue all within a few blocks of each other.
BLM: This island was a Swedish colony for nearly a century before France bought it back in 1878 — its capital, Gustavia, is still named for a Swedish king.
BLR: Nearly 40% of this country is covered in forest, home to some of the last European bison living in the wild.
BLZ: This is the only country in Central America where English is the official language, a legacy of British colonial rule as British Honduras.
BMU: This island isn't in the Caribbean at all — it sits alone in the North Atlantic, over 1,000 km from the nearest Caribbean island.
BOL: This country has two capitals: Sucre is the constitutional capital, while La Paz, the seat of government, sits 3,640 meters up — the highest administrative capital in the world.
BRA: This country shares a border with every other South American country except Chile and Ecuador.
BRB: This country became the world's newest republic in 2021, removing the British monarch as head of state after nearly 400 years.
BRN: This country's sultan has a personal car collection reportedly numbering in the thousands, funded by vast oil and gas reserves.
BTN: This country measures national progress with a 'Gross National Happiness' index instead of GDP, and is one of the only carbon-negative countries on Earth.
BWA: This country is home to more elephants than any other country on Earth, with a population estimated well over 100,000.
CAF: This country sits almost exactly at the geographic center of the African continent.
CAN: This country is home to roughly two million lakes — more than any other country on Earth, covering almost 9% of its total land area.
CHE: This country has enough nuclear fallout shelters to house more than 100% of its population.
CHL: This country stretches over 4,300 km from north to south but averages only about 175 km wide, making it one of the longest, thinnest countries on Earth.
CHN: This country spans five geographic time zones but uses only one official time zone nationwide.
CIV: This country's official capital is Yamoussoukro, but nearly every embassy and most government activity remains in the larger city of Abidjan.
CMR: This country is nicknamed 'Africa in miniature' because it contains nearly every major climate and landscape on the continent, from desert to rainforest to snow-capped peaks.
COD: This country's principal river is the deepest in the world, plunging to depths of over 220 meters in places.
COG: This country's capital sits directly across the river from the capital of its similarly-named neighbor — the closest two capital cities to each other in the world.
COK: This country is self-governing in free association with New Zealand, and residents hold New Zealand citizenship despite issuing their own currency and stamps.
COL: This is the only South American country with coastlines on both the Pacific Ocean and the Caribbean Sea.
COM: This island nation is one of the world's largest producers of ylang-ylang, a flower essential to many high-end perfumes — it's sometimes called the 'Perfume Islands' as a result.
CPV: This island nation was uninhabited until Portuguese sailors settled it in the 15th century, later making it a key hub in the transatlantic slave trade.
CRI: This country abolished its military entirely in 1948 and has remained one of the most stable democracies in Latin America ever since.
CUB: This country's classic American cars from the 1950s are still driven daily, kept running for decades by mechanics who can't legally import new parts.
CUW: This island gave its name to the blue liqueur, originally made from the dried peels of the bitter Laraha orange that grows almost nowhere else.
CYM: This territory has more registered companies than people, thanks to its status as one of the world's largest offshore financial centers.
CYP: This country has been divided since 1974 by a UN buffer zone, and its capital remains the world's last divided capital city.
CZE: This country has the highest per-capita beer consumption in the world, brewing traditions that trace back centuries to towns like Pilsen.
DEU: This country is home to more than 1,500 registered types of sausage, and UNESCO recognized its bread culture — with over 3,000 documented recipes — as Intangible Cultural Heritage in 2014.
DJI: This country hosts military bases from the U.S., France, China, and Japan simultaneously, all within a country smaller than Massachusetts.
DMA: Nicknamed the 'Nature Island,' this country is popularly said to have 365 rivers — one for every day of the year — though the exact number depends on how small a stream has to be to count.
DNK: This country's Lego bricks are precise enough that a brick made in 1958 still fits perfectly with one made today.
DOM: This country and its neighbor share the island of Hispaniola, the only island in the world divided between two independent nations for centuries.
DZA: This is Africa's largest country by land area, and over 80% of it is covered by the Sahara Desert.
ECU: Because of the equatorial bulge, the summit of this country's Mount Chimborazo is the point on Earth's surface farthest from the planet's center — farther out than Everest's peak.
EGY: The Great Pyramid was the tallest human-made structure on Earth for almost 3,800 years — the longest such reign of any building in history.
ERI: This country's capital is a UNESCO World Heritage Site nicknamed 'Africa's Art Deco city' for its remarkably preserved 1930s modernist architecture.
ESH: This territory is Africa's last major unresolved colonial-era territorial dispute, claimed by both Morocco and the Sahrawi independence movement.
ESP: This is the only country with land on both mainland Europe and mainland Africa, via its cities of Ceuta and Melilla.
EST: This country was the first to allow nationwide online voting and remains one of the most digitally governed societies on Earth.
ETH: This country runs on its own calendar, currently several years behind the Gregorian calendar, and marks the new year in September.
FIN: This country has more saunas than cars, with an estimated one sauna for roughly every two people.
FJI: This country is made up of over 330 islands, but only about a third of them are permanently inhabited.
FLK: This territory is home to roughly 500,000 sheep, outnumbering the human population by more than 130 to 1.
FRA: This is the most visited country in the world, welcoming more international tourists annually than any other nation.
FRO: This territory has more sheep than people, and its name literally translates to 'Sheep Islands.'
FSM: This country is spread across more than 600 islands over an expanse of Pacific roughly the size of the continental United States.
GAB: This country set aside roughly 11% of its land as national parkland in a single stroke in 2002, one of the highest shares of protected rainforest in the world.
GBR: 'Big Ben' technically refers only to the bell inside the clock tower, which is officially named Elizabeth Tower.
GEO: This country claims to be the birthplace of wine, with archaeological evidence of winemaking dating back roughly 8,000 years.
GGY: This territory issues its own banknotes and coins, even though it uses the British pound and isn't part of the United Kingdom or the EU.
GHA: This was the first sub-Saharan African country to gain independence from colonial rule, in 1957.
GIN: This country holds some of the world's largest known reserves of bauxite, the ore used to produce aluminum.
GMB: This is mainland Africa's smallest country, a narrow strip of land following its namesake river and almost entirely surrounded by Senegal.
GNB: This country's Bijagos Archipelago is one of the few places in the world where hippos are known to swim in the open ocean between islands.
GNQ: This is the only African country where Spanish is an official language, a legacy of Spanish colonial rule.
GRC: In this country, discovering ancient ruins during construction is common enough that a metro line, apartment building, or airport project can be delayed for years while archaeologists excavate the site first.
GRD: This country is nicknamed the 'Island of Spice' for being one of the world's largest producers of nutmeg, which even appears on its national flag.
GRL: This is the world's largest island, but over 80% of it is permanently covered by an ice sheet.
GTM: This country is home to over 30 volcanoes, several still active, more than any other Central American country.
GUM: This territory is often called 'where America's day begins,' since it sits just west of the international date line among the first U.S. territories to greet each new day.
GUY: This is the only English-speaking country in South America, a legacy of British colonial rule as British Guiana.
HKG: This territory has more skyscrapers than any other city in the world, packing thousands of tall buildings into a famously mountainous, land-scarce territory.
HMD: This remote territory is home to Australia's only active volcano, nicknamed Big Ben, on a territory so remote it's rarely visited more than once a decade.
HND: This country is home to the second-largest barrier reef system in the world, part of the Mesoamerican Reef along its Caribbean coast.
HRV: The necktie traces back to the scarves worn by this country's mercenaries in 17th-century France, whose name gave us the word 'cravat.'
HTI: This country became the first in the world founded by a successful slave revolt, declaring independence from France in 1804.
HUN: The Rubik's Cube was invented in 1974 by Erno Rubik, an architecture professor in this country's capital, originally as a teaching tool for spatial reasoning.
IDN: This is the largest archipelago nation on Earth — its government officially registered 16,056 islands with the United Nations in 2017.
IMN: This territory's parliament, Tynwald, has met continuously since 979 AD, making it one of the oldest continuous parliaments in the world.
IND: This country has more than 20 official languages and hundreds more spoken regionally, yet no single language is designated 'national' in its constitution.
INDIAN-OCEAN-TER: This French-administered island group hosts no permanent civilian population, only rotating military and scientific personnel.
IOT: This territory's Diego Garcia atoll hosts a major joint military base and has no permanent civilian residents.
IRL: This country is nicknamed the 'Emerald Isle' for its intensely green landscape, a product of near-constant Atlantic rainfall.
IRN: This country is home to one of the oldest continuous civilizations on Earth, with the Persian Empire tracing back over 2,500 years.
IRQ: This country sits on the land historically known as Mesopotamia, widely considered the cradle of writing, the wheel, and humanity's earliest cities.
ISL: This country has no standing army and no mosquitoes, one of the only places on Earth confirmed to be mosquito-free.
ISR: The Dead Sea, which it borders, sits at roughly 430 meters below sea level, the lowest land elevation on Earth.
ITA: This country has more UNESCO World Heritage Sites than any other country in the world.
JAM: This country was the birthplace of reggae music and produced two of the fastest sprint times ever recorded, both by the same sprinter.
JEY: This island's tidal range is among the largest in the world, with the sea receding far enough at low tide to walk to neighboring islets.
JOR: This country's ancient city of Petra was carved directly into rose-colored sandstone cliffs and remained largely unknown to the Western world until 1812.
JPN: This country's Geospatial Information Authority recounted its islands using modern digital mapping in 2023 and found 14,125 of them — more than double the previously cited figure of 6,800.
KAZ: This is the world's largest landlocked country by land area, roughly the size of Western Europe.
KEN: This country's Great Rift Valley is part of a tectonic split so large it's slowly pulling East Africa apart, potentially forming a new ocean over millions of years.
KGZ: This country is landlocked and more than 90% mountainous, one of the most vertical countries on Earth.
KHM: This country's Angkor Wat is the largest religious monument in the world by land area, originally built as a Hindu temple before becoming Buddhist.
KIR: This is the only country in the world situated in all four hemispheres — north, south, east, and west.
KNA: This is the smallest sovereign country in the Americas, both by area and by population.
KOR: This country has one of the fastest average internet speeds in the world, a legacy of aggressive early broadband investment in the late 1990s.
KOSOVO: This territory declared independence from Serbia in 2008 and is recognized by roughly half of UN member states, making its sovereignty one of the most contested in the world.
KWT: This country has one of the highest concentrations of proven oil reserves in the world relative to its small land area.
LAO: This is the only landlocked country in Southeast Asia, and parts of it remain littered with unexploded ordnance from the Vietnam War era.
LBN: This is one of the only countries where you can ski in the mountains and swim in the Mediterranean on the same day.
LBR: This country was founded in the 19th century by freed American slaves and modeled its flag and constitution closely on those of the United States.
LBY: This country holds Africa's largest proven oil reserves, despite the Sahara Desert covering roughly 90% of its territory.
LCA: This is the only country in the world named after a woman — Saint Lucy of Syracuse.
LIE: This is one of only two doubly landlocked countries in the world, meaning it's surrounded entirely by other landlocked countries.
LKA: This country was the first in the world to elect a female head of government, Sirimavo Bandaranaike, in 1960.
LSO: This is the only country in the world located entirely above 1,000 meters in elevation, earning it the nickname 'Kingdom in the Sky.'
LTU: This country was once the largest in Europe, when its Grand Duchy stretched from the Baltic Sea nearly to the Black Sea in the 15th century.
LUX: This country has one of the highest GDP-per-capita figures in the world, and public transport across the entire country has been free since 2020.
LVA: This country's capital has one of the largest and best-preserved collections of Art Nouveau buildings in Europe.
MAC: This territory generates more gambling revenue than Las Vegas, making it the largest gambling market in the world.
MAF: This island is split between France and the Netherlands, one of the smallest landmasses in the world shared by two sovereign nations.
MAR: Almost none of the classic film 'Casablanca' was actually filmed in this country's real city of the same name.
MCO: This is the second-smallest country in the world, yet it's more densely populated than any other sovereign nation.
MDA: This country's Milestii Mici wine cellar holds the largest wine collection in the world by bottle count, stretching over 200 km of underground tunnels.
MDG: This island split from mainland Africa roughly 150 million years ago, leaving over 90% of its wildlife found nowhere else on Earth.
MDV: This is the lowest-lying country in the world, with an average ground elevation of just 1.5 meters above sea level.
MEX: Chocolate, corn, tomatoes, and chili peppers all spread to the rest of the world from this country following European contact.
MHL: This country's Bikini Atoll was the site of 23 nuclear tests between 1946 and 1958, and remains uninhabited by its original islanders to this day.
MKD: This country changed its name from simply 'Macedonia' in 2019 to resolve a decades-long naming dispute with its neighbor to the south.
MLI: This country's ancient city of Timbuktu was once one of the wealthiest trading centers in the world, famed for gold, salt, and a legendary library of manuscripts.
MLT: This country's capital is so densely packed with historic buildings that the entire city itself is listed as a single UNESCO World Heritage Site.
MMR: This country was known as Burma until a military government officially renamed it in 1989, a change still not recognized by every country.
MNE: This country's name literally means 'Black Mountain,' referencing the dark, forested peaks that dominate its coastline.
MNG: This is the least densely populated sovereign country on Earth, and roughly a third of its population still lives a nomadic or semi-nomadic herding lifestyle.
MNP: The Mariana Trench, nearby, holds the deepest known point in all of Earth's oceans, the Challenger Deep.
MOZ: This country's coastline stretches for roughly 2,500 km along the Indian Ocean, among the longest of any African country.
MRT: This was the last country in the world to formally criminalize slavery, doing so in law only in 1981 and adding criminal penalties in 2007.
MSR: This territory's capital was buried by volcanic ash after the Soufriere Hills eruptions in the 1990s and remains an abandoned exclusion zone today.
MUS: This island was the only known home of the dodo bird, which went extinct within roughly a century of the island's discovery by sailors.
MWI: A lake here holds more fish species than any other lake in the world, with estimates exceeding 1,000 distinct species.
MYS: This country's Petronas Towers were the tallest buildings in the world when completed in 1998, and remain the tallest twin towers on Earth.
N-CYPRUS: This territory declared independence in 1983 and is recognized by only one UN member state, making it one of the most diplomatically isolated territories on Earth.
NAM: This was the first country in the world to write environmental protection directly into its constitution, in 1990.
NCL: This territory's lagoon is one of the largest in the world and a UNESCO World Heritage Site, home to an extraordinary diversity of marine life.
NER: This country is named for the river running through it, but roughly 80% of its territory lies within the Sahara Desert.
NFK: This island was originally settled to grow flax and pine for the British Navy, then later used as a particularly remote penal colony.
NGA: This country's Nollywood film industry has for years ranked among the two or three largest in the world by number of films produced annually, often surpassing Hollywood in sheer volume.
NIC: This country's largest lake is one of the only freshwater lakes in the world known to contain bull sharks, which adapted to survive without salt water.
NIU: This is one of the world's largest coral islands and has a population small enough that nearly everyone knows each other by name.
NLD: About a quarter of this country sits below sea level, protected by an extensive system of dikes, dunes, and pumps.
NOR: This country's coastline, including all its fjords and islands, stretches over 100,000 km — long enough to circle the Earth roughly two and a half times.
NPL: This is the only country in the world whose national flag isn't rectangular — it's made of two stacked triangular pennants.
NRU: This is the smallest island nation in the world and, for a period in the 1980s, had one of the highest per-capita incomes on Earth from phosphate mining.
NZL: This was the first country in the world to grant women the right to vote in parliamentary elections, in 1893.
OMN: This country's frankincense trade helped make the region one of the ancient world's most valuable trade routes, with the resin once worth its weight in gold.
PAK: This country is home to K2, the second-highest mountain on Earth, and more peaks over 8,000 meters than any country besides Nepal and China.
PAN: The canal here is one of the only places in the world where ships heading from the Atlantic to the Pacific effectively travel from northwest to southeast.
PCN: This territory has one of the smallest populations of any in the world, descended largely from the mutineers of HMS Bounty and their Tahitian companions.
PER: This country's Machu Picchu sits at over 2,400 meters and was unknown to the wider world until its 1911 rediscovery by American historian Hiram Bingham.
PHL: This country is made up of 7,641 islands, officially counted by the national mapping agency (NAMRIA) in 2016 — many of the smallest are still uninhabited and unnamed.
PLW: This country's Jellyfish Lake is home to millions of jellyfish that have lost their sting over thousands of years of evolving without natural predators.
PNG: This is one of the most linguistically diverse places on Earth, with over 800 distinct languages spoken.
POL: This country's Wieliczka Salt Mine has been continuously mined since the 13th century and contains entire chapels carved out of rock salt.
PRI: This territory was once home to the Arecibo Observatory, for decades one of the largest radio telescopes ever built and used in the search for extraterrestrial signals.
PRK: This country and its neighbor to the south remain technically at war, since the 1953 war between them ended with an armistice, not a formal peace treaty.
PRT: This country's Age of Exploration in the 15th century helped launch the first truly global trade networks, connecting Europe, Africa, Asia, and the Americas.
PRY: This country generates nearly all of its domestic electricity from hydropower and is a net exporter of electricity thanks to the massive Itaipu Dam.
PSE: This territory is recognized by a majority of United Nations member states, though it holds only non-member observer status at the UN itself.
PYF: This territory's most famous island is credited as the birthplace of the overwater bungalow, a hospitality concept believed to have originated there in the 1960s.
QAT: This country has one of the highest GDP-per-capita figures in the world, built largely on its vast natural gas reserves.
ROU: This country's Sighișoara, in Transylvania, is the birthplace of Vlad the Impaler — the 15th-century Wallachian ruler whose brutal reputation inspired Dracula.
RUS: This country spans eleven time zones, more than any other in the world.
RWA: This country banned single-use plastic bags nationwide in 2008, well before most of the world began considering similar policies.
SAU: This country has no permanent rivers anywhere within it, one of the largest nations on Earth without a single one.
SDN: This country has more pyramids than Egypt, with over 200 ancient Nubian pyramids scattered across its desert.
SEN: This country's Lake Retba, also called Lac Rose, turns a distinctive pink color due to algae that thrive in its extremely salty water.
SGP: This is one of only three surviving city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.
SGS: This territory has no permanent civilian population but hosts more breeding king penguins than almost anywhere else on Earth.
SHN: This is one of the most remote inhabited islands in the world and was the site of Napoleon Bonaparte's final exile until his death in 1821.
SIACHEN-GLACIER: This glacier, disputed between India and Pakistan, is the highest battlefield on Earth, with troops stationed at altitudes above 6,000 meters.
SLB: This country was the site of some of the fiercest Pacific battles of World War II, including the pivotal Battle of Guadalcanal.
SLE: This country's capital was founded in 1792 as a settlement for freed and formerly enslaved people, which is how the city got its name.
SLV: This became the first country in the world to adopt Bitcoin as legal tender, in 2021.
SMR: This country claims to be the oldest surviving sovereign state and constitutional republic in the world, founded, by tradition, in the year 301 AD.
SOM: This country has the longest coastline of any country on mainland Africa.
SOMALILAND: This territory declared independence from Somalia in 1991 and functions as a de facto independent state, though no UN member country formally recognizes it.
SPM: This territory is the last remaining piece of the once-vast territory of New France still governed by France, just off the coast of Canada.
SRB: This country's capital has reportedly been destroyed and rebuilt around 40 times throughout its history, owing to its position at the confluence of two major rivers — one of the most fought-over city sites in Europe.
SSD: This country became the world's newest in 2011, seceding from Sudan after decades of civil war.
STP: This is Africa's second-smallest country, and its economy was historically built almost entirely on cocoa production.
SUR: This is the smallest sovereign country in South America, and over 90% of its territory remains covered in pristine rainforest.
SVK: This country is home to over 100 castles and around 400 chateaux and manor houses — one of the highest concentrations of historic fortifications in Europe, packed into a country roughly the size of Vermont and New Hampshire combined.
SVN: This country's capital was named European Green Capital in 2016, with more than half the city's area given over to green space.
SWE: This country's legal tradition of Allemansratten, the 'right to roam,' lets anyone walk, camp, or forage on almost any land, public or private.
SWZ: This country changed its name in 2018, when its king officially renamed it to mark 50 years of independence.
SXM: This island is famous for a beach right next to the runway, where jets land so low overhead that tourists gather just to feel the engine blast.
SYC: This country is home to the coco de mer, a palm tree that produces the largest and heaviest seed of any plant on Earth.
SYR: This country's capital is considered one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world, with habitation dating back thousands of years.
TCA: This territory is home to one of the largest barrier reef systems in the world, drawing divers from across the globe.
TCD: This country's namesake lake has shrunk by roughly 90% since the 1960s, once one of the largest lakes in Africa and now a fraction of its former size.
TGO: This is one of the narrowest countries in Africa, stretching just over 50 km wide at its narrowest point.
THA: This is the only country in Southeast Asia never colonized by a European power.
TJK: This is one of the most mountainous countries in the world, with over 90% of its territory covered by mountains.
TKM: This country's Darvaza gas crater, nicknamed the 'Door to Hell,' has been burning in the Karakum Desert for decades — locals still debate exactly when and how it was first ignited.
TLS: This country became Asia's newest in 2002, after gaining full independence following decades of Indonesian and earlier Portuguese rule.
TON: This is the only Pacific island nation never formally colonized by a foreign power. Its monarchy traces back over a thousand years through several ruling dynasties, though the current House of Tupou has reigned since only 1875.
TTO: This is the birthplace of steelpan music, invented from repurposed oil drums in the 20th century.
TUN: This country's desert town of Matmata, with its underground cave dwellings, was used as a filming location for Luke Skywalker's home on Tatooine in Star Wars.
TUR: This country's largest city straddles two continents, split by the Bosphorus Strait between Europe and Asia.
TWN: This economy produces the vast majority of the world's most advanced computer chips, largely through a single company, TSMC.
TZA: This country is home to both the highest point in Africa, Mount Kilimanjaro, and one of the continent's lowest points, the floor of Lake Tanganyika.
UGA: This country is home to more than half of the world's remaining mountain gorillas, primarily in its Bwindi Impenetrable Forest.
UKR: This country's extraordinarily fertile black soil, called chernozem, made it historically known as the 'breadbasket of Europe.'
URY: This was the first country in the world to fully legalize the production, sale, and use of recreational cannabis nationwide, in 2013.
USA: This country has no official national language at the federal level, despite English being the de facto language of government and commerce.
UZB: This is one of only two doubly landlocked countries in the world, meaning every country that borders it is also landlocked.
VAT: This is the smallest sovereign state in the world, with a population under 1,000 and its own train station, postal system, and currency.
VCT: This country's Wallilabou Bay was a filming location for Pirates of the Caribbean, and its film-set dock structures still stand today.
VEN: This country's Angel Falls is the tallest uninterrupted waterfall in the world, dropping nearly 980 meters from the top of a flat-topped mountain called a tepui.
VGB: This territory has more registered offshore companies than people, a legacy of its role as a major international financial center.
VIR: This U.S. territory drives on the left side of the road, despite nearly everywhere else in the country driving on the right.
VNM: This is the world's second-largest coffee exporter, behind only Brazil, thanks to French colonial-era plantations that took root in its highlands.
VUT: This country's Mount Yasur is one of the most accessible active volcanoes on Earth, letting visitors stand near its erupting crater rim.
WLF: This is one of the least-visited territories on Earth, lacking an airport capable of handling large international flights until relatively recently.
WSM: This country skipped December 30, 2011 entirely, jumping forward a full day to switch sides of the International Date Line and align with its main trading partners.
YEM: This country's Socotra archipelago is home to the alien-looking dragon's blood tree, found nowhere else on Earth and named for its deep red sap.
ZAF: This country has three capital cities — Pretoria, Cape Town, and Bloemfontein — each hosting a different branch of government.
ZMB: Victoria Falls, shared with a neighboring country, is classified as the largest sheet of falling water in the world by combined height and width.
ZWE: This country takes its name from Great Zimbabwe, a medieval stone city whose ruins are the largest ancient stone structure in sub-Saharan Africa — built entirely without mortar.
